---
title: "01 · Functions as Machines"
subtitle: "Input goes in, output comes out — and the rule between them is the function"
lesson-id: "t2.functions-as-machines"
tier: 2
prerequisites: ["t1.intro-variables"]
unlocks: ["t2.linear-functions-graphs"]
applied-domains: ["ai-ml", "research"]
estimated-minutes: 30
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Have you done [Tier 1, Lesson 11 — Variables](../../tier-1-foundations/11-intro-variables/index.qmd)?
2. Can you compute $2x + 5$ for $x = 3$ and get 11?
3. Have you used a vending machine — put in money, pressed a button,
   got a fixed item? That is the function idea, exactly.
:::

## Touch

Pick a *machine* (a rule). Pick an *input*. See the output.

```{ojs}
viewof rule = Inputs.select(["double it (×2)", "add 7 (+7)", "square it (x²)", "halve it (÷2)"], {value: "double it (×2)", label: "Machine:"})
viewof inp = Inputs.range([-10, 10], {value: 3, step: 1, label: "Input:"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  let out, ruleSym;
  switch(rule) {
    case "double it (×2)":  out = 2*inp;     ruleSym = "f(x) = 2x"; break;
    case "add 7 (+7)":      out = inp + 7;   ruleSym = "f(x) = x + 7"; break;
    case "square it (x²)":  out = inp*inp;   ruleSym = "f(x) = x²"; break;
    case "halve it (÷2)":   out = inp/2;     ruleSym = "f(x) = x ÷ 2"; break;
  }
  const W = 720, H = 180, pad = 40;
  const svg = d3.create("svg").attr("viewBox", `0 0 ${W} ${H}`)
    .attr("style", "width:100%; max-width:760px;");
  // Input
  svg.append("rect").attr("x", pad).attr("y", H/2 - 30).attr("width", 100).attr("height", 60)
    .attr("fill", "#90caf9").attr("stroke", "#2c5f7c").attr("stroke-width", 2).attr("rx", 6);
  svg.append("text").attr("x", pad + 50).attr("y", H/2 + 8)
    .attr("text-anchor", "middle").attr("font-size", "26").attr("font-weight", "bold").text(`${inp}`);
  svg.append("text").attr("x", pad + 50).attr("y", H/2 - 40)
    .attr("text-anchor", "middle").attr("font-size", "13").attr("fill", "#666").text("input x");
  // Arrow + machine
  svg.append("line").attr("x1", pad + 105).attr("x2", pad + 175)
    .attr("y1", H/2).attr("y2", H/2).attr("stroke", "#1f2933").attr("stroke-width", 2);
  svg.append("path").attr("d", `M ${pad+170},${H/2} l -8,-5 l 0,10 z`).attr("fill", "#1f2933");
  svg.append("rect").attr("x", pad + 180).attr("y", H/2 - 50).attr("width", 280).attr("height", 100)
    .attr("fill", "#fffde7").attr("stroke", "#f9a825").attr("stroke-width", 2).attr("rx", 6);
  svg.append("text").attr("x", pad + 320).attr("y", H/2 + 6)
    .attr("text-anchor", "middle").attr("font-size", "20").attr("font-family", "monospace").text(ruleSym);
  svg.append("text").attr("x", pad + 320).attr("y", H/2 - 60)
    .attr("text-anchor", "middle").attr("font-size", "13").attr("fill", "#666").text("the machine f");
  // Out arrow
  svg.append("line").attr("x1", pad + 465).attr("x2", pad + 535)
    .attr("y1", H/2).attr("y2", H/2).attr("stroke", "#1f2933").attr("stroke-width", 2);
  svg.append("path").attr("d", `M ${pad+530},${H/2} l -8,-5 l 0,10 z`).attr("fill", "#1f2933");
  svg.append("rect").attr("x", pad + 540).attr("y", H/2 - 30).attr("width", 100).attr("height", 60)
    .attr("fill", "#a5d6a7").attr("stroke", "#2e7d32").attr("stroke-width", 2).attr("rx", 6);
  svg.append("text").attr("x", pad + 590).attr("y", H/2 + 8)
    .attr("text-anchor", "middle").attr("font-size", "26").attr("font-weight", "bold")
    .text(`${out % 1 === 0 ? out : out.toFixed(1)}`);
  svg.append("text").attr("x", pad + 590).attr("y", H/2 - 40)
    .attr("text-anchor", "middle").attr("font-size", "13").attr("fill", "#666").text("output f(x)");
  return svg.node();
}
```

The machine is a **rule** that converts each input into exactly one
output. We give it a name (here, $f$). The notation $f(3)$ means "feed
3 into $f$ and read the output."

## Wonder

1. Pick the "double it" machine. What output does the input 0 give?
   Input −5? Input 100?
2. Could one input give *two* different outputs? Why or why not?
3. Could two different inputs give the *same* output? (Try the
   "square it" machine with inputs 3 and −3.)

## Discover

### Problem 1

For $f(x) = 3x - 1$, compute $f(2)$, $f(0)$, $f(-4)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Substitute the value of $x$ into the rule.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$f(2) = 3 \cdot 2 - 1 = 5$.
$f(0) = 3 \cdot 0 - 1 = -1$.
$f(-4) = 3 \cdot (-4) - 1 = -13$.

Each input gives exactly one output. That is the *defining* property
of a function.
</details>

### Problem 2

The "square it" machine $g(x) = x^2$ produces $g(3) = 9$ and $g(-3) = 9$
— two different inputs gave the same output. Is that allowed?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Yes.** A function is allowed to send different inputs to the *same*
output. What it *can't* do is send a single input to two different
outputs. (Functions are *one-to-one* about which output each input
gets, not about whether outputs can be reused.)

The "square" machine even has a special property — it loses
information. If you only know the output 9, you can't tell whether the
input was 3 or −3. We will return to this when we meet **inverse
functions** in Tier 3.
</details>

### Problem 3

A taxi charges Rs. 30 base + Rs. 15 per km. Write a function $C(d)$
that gives cost as a function of distance $d$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
This is the formula from Tier 1, Lesson 11 — now with function
notation.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$$C(d) = 30 + 15d.$$

$C$ is the *function name*, $d$ is the *input variable*, and $30 + 15d$
is the *rule*. To find the cost for 8 km: $C(8) = 30 + 15 \cdot 8 =
150$. The notation makes "input goes in, output comes out" explicit.
</details>

### Problem 4

For the function $f(x) = x^2$, what is the largest possible input? The
smallest? What are the possible *outputs*?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Inputs: any real number. Outputs: think about what $x^2$ can produce.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Inputs:** any real number — there's no input that breaks $x^2$.
**Outputs:** $x^2 \ge 0$ always, so outputs are *non-negative* (zero
or positive). The output **2** would require $x = \sqrt{2}$ (a real
number), but the output **−2** would require $x^2 = -2$, which has no
real solution.

The set of allowed inputs is called the **domain**. The set of possible
outputs is called the **range**. For $f(x) = x^2$: domain = all real
numbers, range = $[0, \infty)$.
</details>

### Problem 5

A function $h$ is given as a *table*:

| $x$ | $h(x)$ |
|:-:|:-:|
| 0 | 5 |
| 1 | 7 |
| 2 | 9 |
| 3 | 11 |

Can you guess the rule? What is $h(10)$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
As $x$ goes up by 1, $h(x)$ goes up by 2. And when $x = 0$, $h(x) = 5$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$h(x) = 2x + 5$. Check: $h(0) = 5$ ✓, $h(1) = 7$ ✓, etc.
$h(10) = 2 \cdot 10 + 5 = 25$.

Functions can be specified in many ways: as a **rule** (formula),
as a **table** of input-output pairs, or — as we will see in Lesson 5
— as a **graph**. All three are different costumes for the same idea.
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
The modern definition of a function — *any rule assigning each input to
exactly one output* — was hammered out in the 19th century by **Dirichlet**
(1837) and **Frege** (1879), who broke the older idea that a function had to
be given by a formula. Before them, mathematicians like Euler still expected
every function to be writeable as an algebraic expression. The "rule of any
kind" view is what makes functions general enough to underpin modern logic,
computer science, and machine learning.
:::

::: {.key-insight}
A function is a *promise*: hand it any allowed input, and it will hand back
exactly one output — every time. The rule is fixed; the input varies; the
machine does the rest.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

A **function** $f$ is a rule that assigns to each input value exactly
**one** output value. We write
$$f(x) = \ldots$$
where $x$ is the input (often called the **argument**) and $f(x)$ is
the output. The expression on the right is the **rule**.

The set of allowed inputs is the **domain**. The set of possible
outputs is the **range**.

To **evaluate** a function at a specific input is to substitute the
input into the rule and compute the output. $f(3)$ means "evaluate $f$
at 3."

A function can be specified as:

- a **formula** (rule): $f(x) = 2x + 5$,
- a **table** of input/output pairs,
- a **graph** (picture, in Lesson 5),
- a **description** in words ("doubles the input, then adds 5").

:::

> *One input, one output — that is a function;*
> *Substitute the input into the rule;*
> *Domain says what inputs are allowed; range says what outputs appear.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> Let $f(x) = x^2 - 2x + 1$. Compute $f(0), f(1), f(3), f(-2)$.

A competent solver substitutes carefully:

- $f(0) = 0^2 - 2 \cdot 0 + 1 = 1$.
- $f(1) = 1 - 2 + 1 = 0$.
- $f(3) = 9 - 6 + 1 = 4$.
- $f(-2) = 4 - (-4) + 1 = 4 + 4 + 1 = 9$.

Pay attention to **signs** when substituting negatives — wrap the
input in brackets so the squaring sign and the multiplication sign
don't get confused.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** $f(x) = 4x - 3$. Compute $f(0), f(2), f(-1)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$-3, 5, -7$.
</details>

**W2.** $g(t) = t^2 + 1$. Compute $g(0), g(3), g(-3)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$1, 10, 10$. (Both $\pm 3$ give the same output — see Discover P2.)
</details>

**W3.** A function takes the input *Tuesday* and outputs *2*. The
function takes *Wednesday* and outputs *3*. What is the function?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
"Day of the week → day-number, Sunday = 1." Functions don't have to
take numbers as inputs! Anything that maps each input to exactly one
output is a function.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** A function $f$ has $f(2) = 7$ and $f(5) = 19$. Could the rule
be $f(x) = 4x - 1$? Verify.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$f(2) = 4 \cdot 2 - 1 = 7$ ✓. $f(5) = 4 \cdot 5 - 1 = 19$ ✓.
**Yes** — the rule is consistent with both data points.

(That doesn't *prove* the rule is $4x - 1$ — many other rules pass
through the same two points. But it is consistent. To pin down a
*linear* rule, two points are enough — that's the topic of Lesson 5.)
</details>

**P2.** A water tank starts with **500 litres** and is being drained at
**12 litres per minute**. Write a function $V(t)$ that gives the volume
remaining after $t$ minutes. After how many minutes is the tank empty?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Volume at time 0 = 500. Volume decreases by 12 per minute.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$V(t) = 500 - 12t$. Tank is empty when $V(t) = 0$:
$500 - 12t = 0 \Rightarrow t = \dfrac{500}{12} \approx 41.7$ minutes.

The function captures *both* the initial state (500 at $t = 0$) and
the rate of change (−12 per minute) in one tidy expression.
</details>

**P3.** $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{x}$. What is $f(2)$? What is $f(0)$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$f(2) = 1/2$. For $f(0)$ — division by zero!
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$f(2) = 0.5$. $f(0)$ is **undefined** — division by zero is not
allowed (Tier 1, Lesson 6).

So the **domain** of $f(x) = 1/x$ is "all real numbers *except* 0".
Real-world functions often have such restrictions. Knowing the domain
matters as much as knowing the rule.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** A neural network in machine learning, at its simplest, is a
function: $\hat{y} = f(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n)$ — many inputs, one
output. The inputs are *features* (e.g. pixel values of an image), and
the output is a *prediction* (e.g. "this is a cat" with confidence
0.87). Why is the function abstraction perfect for ML?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Because ML asks "given inputs, what is the most likely output?"** —
the exact question a function answers. Training a neural network is
the process of *finding* a function $f$ such that $f(x) \approx y$ for
all known input–output pairs $(x, y)$ in the training data.

The whole field of supervised learning is "learn a function from
examples". Tier 1 Lesson 11 introduced the algebraic side; Tier 2
Lesson 1 introduces the function side; Tier 5+ will build the deep
learning machinery on this foundation.
</details>

**S2.** A function $f$ has the property that $f(f(x)) = x$ for *every*
input $x$. (Applying $f$ twice gives back what you started with.) Find
two different rules for $f$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
"Multiply by −1": $f(x) = -x$. Check: $f(f(x)) = -(-x) = x$ ✓.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Two examples:

- $f(x) = -x$. Then $f(f(x)) = -(-x) = x$. ✓
- $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{x}$ (for $x \neq 0$). Then $f(f(x)) =
  \dfrac{1}{1/x} = x$. ✓

A function that is its own inverse is called an **involution**.
Reflections, rotations by 180°, and "negating" are all involutions —
they undo themselves. Involutions are everywhere in geometry and
abstract algebra.
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"$f(x)$ means $f$ times $x$."** No — it means "the function $f$
   evaluated at $x$." The brackets here are *not* multiplication; they
   are *application*. If you want multiplication, write $f \cdot x$.
2. **"A function must have a formula."** No — it just needs to assign
   exactly one output to each input. A table, a graph, or a verbal
   rule all qualify.
3. **"Two different inputs can't give the same output."** They can —
   the squaring function maps both 3 and −3 to 9. The rule is *one
   output per input*, not *one input per output*.
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

1. **A [function](/glossary.qmd#function) is a machine: one input in,
   exactly one output out.**
2. **To evaluate $f(3)$, substitute the input into the rule and compute —
   the brackets mean *application*, not multiplication.**
3. **Same idea, many costumes: a function can be a formula, a table, a
   graph, or a description in words.**

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you explain the difference between $f(x)$ and $f \cdot x$?
2. Without scrolling back, can you compute $f(4)$ for $f(x) = x^2 - 3x
   + 2$?
3. Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 *Discover* problems and 4 of the 6
   practice problems without peeking past *Hint 1*?

(Answer: $f(4) = 16 - 12 + 2 = 6$.)

- **3 yes:** ready for **[02 · Linear Equations](../02-linear-equations-one-var/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo **S2** — involutions are a beautiful idea worth
  feeling.
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover P2 and P5*.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[T1 L11 — Variables](../../tier-1-foundations/11-intro-variables/index.qmd)** —
  function notation $f(x)$ formalises the input–output picture from
  Tier 1.

### What this unlocks

- **[T2 L05 — Linear Functions and Their Graphs](../05-linear-functions-graphs/index.qmd)** —
  visualising functions as pictures on the coordinate plane.
- **Tier 4 — Calculus** — functions are the *objects* calculus studies.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / Machine Learning.** Every model is a function. Training is
finding the function that fits the data.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Mathematical research.** Functions are the central objects of
analysis, topology, abstract algebra (homomorphisms are functions
between algebraic structures), and category theory (where everything
is essentially a function).
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Everywhere else.** Pricing rules, tax brackets, scoring rubrics,
spreadsheet formulas, computer programs (every routine is a function
in this sense). The function abstraction is the language of *rules*.
:::
